title: AML Agents Briefing — Friday, 26 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing. It's Thursday the 26th of June 2026. I'm Alex, joined as always by Jordan. This is your audio overview of today's most material APAC AML and financial crime developments. Full detail, the obligation register, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got a significant warning from the Bank for International Settlements on stablecoins, a major acquisition reshaping Japan's crypto sector, and a set of obligations that should be on every MLRO's radar right now. Let's get into it.

ALEX: Starting with the lead story. The Bank for International Settlements has issued a formal warning that stablecoins carry inherent structural flaws capable of undermining financial stability at scale. This isn't a discussion paper. It's a supervisory-grade finding, and it materially strengthens the hand of regulators across every jurisdiction currently debating how to licence and supervise virtual asset service providers.

JORDAN: What's the BIS specifically flagging here, Alex? Is this about reserve backing, or something deeper?

ALEX: Both, really. The BIS concern runs to design architecture: the redemption mechanics under stress, the opacity of reserve composition, and the systemic contagion risk if a large stablecoin loses its peg during a broader market dislocation. It's the same fault lines we saw in 2022, but now formalised at the level of the central bank for central banks.

JORDAN: And the read-across for compliance teams is significant. If you're a virtual asset service provider operating under MAS Notice PSN02, or you're a licensed exchange under Hong Kong's anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing ordinance, your regulator has just been handed a BIS-backed rationale to tighten stablecoin-specific customer due diligence requirements and transaction monitoring thresholds. Review your stablecoin exposure in your customer book this week. If you haven't stress-tested your KYT vendor's coverage of stablecoin flows, that conversation needs to happen before the end of the quarter.

ALEX: Moving on. SBI Holdings has agreed to acquire Bitbank, one of Japan's registered crypto exchanges, for approximately 42 billion yen, which is around 289 million US dollars. This is one of the largest consolidations in Japan's regulated virtual asset sector to date.

JORDAN: So what does this mean practically for AML compliance teams?

ALEX: It means a major regulated financial conglomerate, subject to Japan's Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds and full FSA supervision, is absorbing a crypto exchange into its group structure. Bitbank will now inherit SBI's compliance infrastructure, but also SBI's regulatory scrutiny.

JORDAN: The action item here is about group-level risk. If you're the MLRO or head of financial crime compliance at a Japanese financial institution, or at a foreign bank with Japanese correspondent relationships, consolidation events like this are a trigger to refresh your correspondent due diligence. Review how you're assessing the AML programme maturity of crypto-affiliated group entities. Group-wide risk assessments need to reflect the combined entity's exposure, not just the legacy banking arm.

ALEX: Elsewhere in the region, the Hong Kong Institute of Bankers Annual Banking Conference is taking place later this month, with overlapping compliance demands across digital assets, cross-border data flows, and AML obligations squarely on the agenda. For senior compliance leaders in Hong Kong, this is worth tracking for supervisory signals, particularly given the HKMA's active posture on virtual asset oversight right now.

JORDAN: And speaking of Hong Kong obligations, the HKMA AML and CFT Guideline, specifically paragraph 11, requires submission of the annual self-assessment return covering the 2024 reporting period by the 30th of September. That's the prescribed Excel template, and it requires independent validation from Internal Audit. If your Internal Audit team hasn't been briefed, get that scheduled now. September seems far away, but the validation cycle takes time.

ALEX: Also on obligations, Indonesian reporting entities should be tracking the requirement under POJK 12 of 2017 for an annual independent review of the AML and CFT programme covering the 2024 reporting period, with findings presented to the Risk Oversight Committee and the Dewan Komisaris, the Board of Commissioners. That deadline falls on the 30th of June.

JORDAN: If you're in-scope in Indonesia, that's four days away. Confirm with your compliance team today that the review findings are documented and that the board presentation is scheduled.

ALEX: Now for what to watch. Three items from our horizon scanning deserve attention in the coming week. First, AUSTRAC has published detailed Tranche 2 guidance specifically for legal practitioners, covering customer engagement triggers, source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing transactions, and legal professional privilege carve-outs for suspicious matter reports. Second, Bank Negara Malaysia has issued a typology bulletin on investment scams routed through digital banks and crypto exchanges, with mule account indicators including rapid post-onboarding velocity spikes and inbound transfers from multiple unrelated retail senders. Third, AUSTRAC has confirmed a civil penalty of 47 million Australian dollars against a registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures in transaction monitoring, mule account detection, and suspicious matter report timeliness.

JORDAN: Three concrete next steps for your coming week. One: if you're an AML and CTF compliance officer at an Australian Tranche 2 entity, pull the AUSTRAC legal practitioner guidance and map it against your current customer risk assessment triggers. Two: for fraud and AML leads at digital banks operating in Malaysia, run the BNM mule account indicators against your onboarding velocity rules before your next transaction monitoring governance meeting. Three: for any digital currency exchange registered with AUSTRAC, the 47 million dollar penalty is your benchmark. Pull your last two quarters of suspicious matter report timeliness data and test it against your regulatory obligations before your next board or risk committee.

ALEX: Full detail on all of today's stories, the obligation register, and horizon scanning is at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: That's your briefing for the 26th of June. Stay sharp out there.

ALEX: We'll be back tomorrow.
